perception.who_is_this_for_a: Used-car buyers who want one official Carfax report quickly, plus dealerships comparing volume pricing.
perception.who_is_this_for_b: Used-car buyers seeking a very cheap official Carfax report, and dealers who want to talk to named account managers or start a trial.

reasoning: Candidate A better matches the required high-trust dual-path landing page. Its first desktop viewport clearly shows official Carfax reports, savings, individual VIN purchase, dealer pricing, Stripe/refund/report-volume trust cues, and a sample report link. The offer feels more restrained and credible, and the dealer path includes visible volume pricing rather than only a call. Candidate B has stronger mobile self-selection and a more explicit human-contact strategy, but the rendered page relies heavily on placeholder business identity, placeholder phone numbers, placeholder names, and photo placeholders directly in the first viewport. For a skeptical buyer, those undermine the very trust they are meant to create. B also leads with a very aggressive $9.99 was $44.99 discount, which may increase scam perception unless supported by real proof. Overall, A is less visually expressive but more credible, balanced, and conversion-ready from what is actually rendered.
blocking_visual_issue_a: Mobile first viewport makes the dealer path less prominent than desktop, and later legitimacy sections contain placeholder legal-entity details.
blocking_visual_issue_b: Placeholder company name, phone numbers, account-manager names, emails, and photo slots are visible in the first viewport, making the page feel unfinished or potentially fake.
